%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Discrete-Time Convolution

> **Section Objective:** Learn exactly what MATLAB conv returns for discrete-time convolution, including output length, support, and validity limits.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- finite-duration convolution
- output length
- region of support
- causal infinite-duration signals
- valid conv samples
- zero-state response by convolution

## Rule 1: Output Length for Finite-Duration Convolution

When \(w\) and \(v\) are finite vectors of lengths \(L_w\) and \(L_v\), their convolution \(w*v\) has exactly \(L_w + L_v - 1\) samples.

**Textbook example:** Two length-4 rectangular pulses produce \(4 + 4 - 1 = 7\) output samples.

```
conv([1 1 1 1],[1 1 1 1]) → [1 2 3 4 3 2 1]
```

The rising-then-falling triangle shape reflects how many samples overlap as one pulse slides past the other.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Always compute output length before worrying about indices.

### COMMON MISTAKE

- Saying output length is \(L_w + L_v\) (forgetting the \(-1\)).
- Assuming MATLAB conv automatically knows the time origin — it does not.

$$\operatorname{length}(w*v) = L_w + L_v - 1$$

## Rule 2: Starting Index of the Convolution Output

MATLAB's conv command returns the numeric values of the convolution but does **not** track the time-axis labels. You must compute the starting index manually.

- \(n_w\) = starting index of vector \(w\)
- \(n_v\) = starting index of vector \(v\)
- \(n_{w*v,\,\mathrm{start}}\) = starting index of the output

**Example 1:** Both length-4 pulses begin at \(n = 0\). Output support: \(0 \le n \le 6\).

**Example 2:** First pulse begins at \(n_w = -4\), second at \(n_v = 0\). The numeric output is still \([1\;2\;3\;4\;3\;2\;1]\), but the support is now \(-4 \le n \le 2\).

### EXAM TRIGGER

Same vector values can carry completely different time labels depending on where each input starts.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Treating \([1\;2\;3\;4\;3\;2\;1]\) as always starting at \(n = 0\).

$$n_{w*v,\,\mathrm{start}} = n_w + n_v$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## Rule 3: Validity Limit for Causal Infinite-Duration Signals

Computers cannot store infinite-duration signals. When you pass the first \(N\) samples of each causal signal into conv, MATLAB returns \(2N - 1\) samples — but only the first \(N\) are mathematically reliable.

**Why?** The later samples depend on future values of the infinite-duration signals that were never supplied. MATLAB computed them from incomplete data.

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Samples supplied per signal | \(N\) |
| Samples conv returns | \(2N - 1\) |
| Valid samples | \(N\) |
| Invalid samples | \(N - 1\) |

### EXAM TRIGGER

When both signals are causal and infinite-duration but only the first \(N\) samples are used, keep only the first \(N\) conv outputs.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Plotting all \(2N - 1\) samples and trusting the decaying tail — the tail looks plausible but is wrong.

$$\operatorname{conv}(h[0{:}N-1],\,x[0{:}N-1]) \Rightarrow \text{only the first } N \text{ samples are valid}$$

## Zero-State Response by Convolution

The zero-state response of a discrete-time LTI system equals the convolution of the impulse response \(h[n]\) with the input \(x[n]\).

**MATLAB practice:** Feed sampled versions of \(h[n]\) and \(x[n]\) into conv. The formula tells you what to convolve — but the valid-sample rule still controls how much of the output you can trust.

**Textbook example:** To find the response over \(0 \le n \le 30\), use the first 31 valid conv outputs. Discard the remaining \(N - 1\) samples.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Assuming the entire conv output is correct just because the command returned it without error. MATLAB does not warn you about truncation.

$$y[n] = h[n] * x[n]$$

## Textbook Example: Impulse Response

This is the impulse response used in the textbook example. You do not need to derive it here — focus on how to use it.

The factor \(u[n]\) makes \(h[n]\) **causal**: it is zero for \(n < 0\) and nonzero for \(n \ge 0\). The input is \(x[n] = \cos(2\pi n / 6)\,u[n]\), which is also causal.

**Use case:** Sample both \(h[n]\) and \(x[n]\) at \(n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1\) and pass them into conv to compute the zero-state response.

### EXAM TRIGGER

The presence of \(u[n]\) means the signal starts at \(n = 0\). It does **not** make the signal finite-duration.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Confusing **causal** (starts at \(n = 0\)) with **finite-duration** (has a definite end). A causal signal can still be infinite in length.

$$h[n] = \left\{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{3}\right) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{3}\right)\right\}u[n]$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Output length rule: \(\operatorname{length}(w*v) = L_w + L_v - 1\); for two length-4 pulses, output has 7 samples.
- Starting index rule: \(n_{w*v,\,\mathrm{start}} = n_w + n_v\); MATLAB conv returns values only — you supply the time labels.
- Validity rule: \(\operatorname{conv}(h[0{:}N-1],\,x[0{:}N-1])\) returns \(2N-1\) samples but only the first \(N\) are valid.
- Zero-state response: \(y[n] = h[n] * x[n]\); feed sampled \(h[n]\) and \(x[n]\) into conv, then keep only the first \(N\) outputs.
- Textbook impulse response \(h[n] = \{\cos(\pi n/3) + (1/\sqrt{3})\sin(\pi n/3)\}u[n]\) is causal but infinite-duration; \(u[n]\) does not make it finite.

*Next, use these rules to decide which part of a computed convolution is mathematically trustworthy.*
